logs.summary: The Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has become the fastest-growing ever, with cases surging above 1,000 and the death toll reaching 600. The outbreak has spread to new provinces including Tshopo and Haut-Uele, and the first case outside Africa has been confirmed in France. Response efforts are hampered by funding shortages, unpaid health workers, and the closure of USAID, while new diagnostic tests and treatment trials offer hope.

logs.summary:
As of July 19, confirmed Ebola cases in the DRC have risen to 2,423 with 967 deaths and 469 recoveries, while 734 patients remain in quarantine or treatment. South Africa has introduced mandatory health screening for travelers arriving from DRC and Uganda in response to the outbreak. The WHO reports signs of stabilization but warns of continued spread in displacement camps.
A UK humanitarian worker who was potentially exposed to the Ebola virus while working in the Democratic Republic of Congo has been medically evacuated to London. The worker is being monitored at a London hospital after arriving on an emergency flight, adding to the international response and case management efforts.
As of July 22, the Ebola outbreak in DRC and Uganda has surpassed 1,000 deaths, with 2,473 confirmed cases (999 deaths) in DRC and 20 cases (2 deaths) in Uganda. Health workers report declining patient numbers due to mistrust and misinformation. A UK humanitarian worker has been medically evacuated to London for monitoring, and a pharmaceutical company has applied for approval to conduct a Phase II clinical trial for an Ebola treatment.
The World Health Organization has indicated that the true scale of the Ebola outbreak in DR Congo and Uganda could be two to four times larger than official figures, which as of late July show over 2,473 cases and 1,000 deaths. The outbreak, caused by the Bundibugyo virus with no approved vaccines or treatments, has been declared the fastest-growing in history, with mistrust and misinformation hindering containment efforts.

incidents.summary:
Israel has ended its on-site rescue mission in Venezuela but will continue to assist remotely. Death toll reports from the selected news vary, with one source citing 5,119 and another 5,069. Meanwhile, hundreds of pets rescued from the rubble are being reunited with their families.
The World Bank reported that the twin earthquakes caused $19.6 billion in direct physical damage, with total rebuilding costs potentially reaching twice that amount. In other developments, a mother is seeking justice for her son who was deported from the U.S. and died in the quakes a day later, a shelter continues reuniting rescued pets with owners, and a Mexican soccer club launched a solidarity campaign for Venezuela.
A World Bank report released July 23 estimates the twin earthquakes caused $19.6 billion in direct physical damage, with total rebuilding costs potentially reaching twice that amount, and the death toll has risen to 5,398. Meanwhile, the Venezuelan diaspora in Mexico has mobilized aid efforts, and soccer matches are providing a temporary distraction for affected communities.

logs.summary:
As of July 21, the death toll from the June 24 twin earthquakes has risen to 5,278. DP World and partners delivered over 27 tonnes of humanitarian aid to Venezuela.
The official death toll from the June 24 twin earthquakes in Venezuela has risen to 5,346, according to National Assembly President Jorge Rodríguez on July 21. New reports detail ongoing search efforts, including a grandfather searching for his missing 9-year-old grandson and two teenage boys digging daily for their mother's body, while a cat was rescued from rubble as a "ray of hope." Franklin Graham visited a Samaritan's Purse field hospital providing orthopedic surgeries and prayer for victims.
A new report from El País details the isolation of coastal towns such as Chichiriviche de la Costa, Puerto Cruz, and Cepe since the June 24 earthquake, highlighting the ongoing impact beyond the main affected areas. Other selected news includes a football match providing a distraction and an opinion piece on earthquake safety, but no new casualty or rescue updates are reported.

logs.summary:
Israel has ended its on-site rescue mission in Venezuela but will continue to assist remotely. Death toll reports from the selected news vary, with one source citing 5,119 and another 5,069. Meanwhile, hundreds of pets rescued from the rubble are being reunited with their families.
The World Bank reported that the twin earthquakes caused $19.6 billion in direct physical damage, with total rebuilding costs potentially reaching twice that amount. In other developments, a mother is seeking justice for her son who was deported from the U.S. and died in the quakes a day later, a shelter continues reuniting rescued pets with owners, and a Mexican soccer club launched a solidarity campaign for Venezuela.
A World Bank report released July 23 estimates the twin earthquakes caused $19.6 billion in direct physical damage, with total rebuilding costs potentially reaching twice that amount, and the death toll has risen to 5,398. Meanwhile, the Venezuelan diaspora in Mexico has mobilized aid efforts, and soccer matches are providing a temporary distraction for affected communities.

logs.summary: A fast-moving wildfire in southern Spain's Almería province, starting around July 9-10, 2026, killed at least 13 people and left over 20 missing, many of them foreign nationals (including British and Belgian). The fire, which prompted evacuations of roughly 800 people, is suspected to have been ignited by a broken power line amid a severe summer heatwave. It is considered one of Spain's deadliest wildfires on record.

logs.summary:
A wildfire erupted in the historic Fontainebleau forest south of Paris, burning over 2,000 hectares and prompting the evacuation of about 1,000 residents. French authorities arrested two people suspected of deliberately starting the blaze, while hundreds of firefighters and waterbombing aircraft worked to contain the fire as Europe faced another heatwave.

The wildfires in the Fontainebleau forest have shown mixed status: some reports indicate containment, while others report the fire is still spreading with 850 firefighters and 200 vehicles deployed. Authorities discovered ten ignition points and arrested two suspects on suspicion of arson.

On July 15, approximately 800 firefighters continued to tackle flare-ups in the Fontainebleau forest as French President Emmanuel Macron pledged to replant the historic forest. Meanwhile, authorities are investigating a volunteer firefighter as a suspect in starting the devastating blaze, which burned over 2,000 hectares near Paris.

incidents.summary:
A powerful magnitude 7.3–7.4 earthquake struck off the coast of Mexico's southern Chiapas state on July 17, 2026, prompting a tsunami warning for nearby coasts of Mexico and Guatemala, with waves up to one meter possible. People evacuated buildings in affected areas, while authorities confirmed no tsunami threat to Hawaii.
A powerful earthquake of magnitude 7.3–7.4 struck off the coast of Chiapas, Mexico, near Puerto Madero on July 17, 2026, at a shallow depth of about 10 km. The quake triggered a tsunami warning and was strongly felt in Guatemala and El Salvador, prompting evacuations in some areas. No immediate damage or fatalities were reported, though a series of aftershocks were recorded.
New reports indicate that the 7.3-magnitude earthquake off the coast of Chiapas, Mexico, caused two injuries and cracked some buildings, though no major damage or fatalities were reported.

logs.summary: A 5.5-magnitude earthquake struck Peru's central Junín region on July 19, 2026, killing at least five people and injuring more than 20, with around 300 displaced. Rescue crews searched rubble for a trapped person and continued to assess damage as aftershocks occurred. Reports varied on the exact death toll, with some sources citing up to six deaths and 32 injuries.
